learning_objectives: Understand the role of an OS as resource manager and abstraction layer
Identify the major components of an operating system
Distinguish between kernel mode and user mode execution

lecture_notes.introduction: The operating system is the most important software on any computer. It manages hardware resources, provides abstractions for application programmers, and enforces security boundaries. Understanding OS internals makes you a dramatically better systems programmer.
key_concepts: OS roles: resource manager, abstraction provider, security enforcer
Kernel mode vs user mode: hardware privilege levels
System calls: the interface between applications and the kernel
OS structure: monolithic kernel, microkernel, hybrid approaches
History: batch systems, time-sharing, modern multi-core systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use strace on Linux to observe the system calls made by a simple hello world program, revealing the hidden OS interactions behind every program

review.value: The CPU hardware enforces two privilege levels: kernel mode can run any instruction and access any memory; user mode is restricted. System calls are the controlled gateway from user mode to kernel mode
A system call requires a mode switch (user to kernel and back), register saving and restoring, and argument validation; this overhead is why minimizing system calls matters for performance

learning_objectives: Understand process creation, states, and context switching
Implement multi-threaded programs using POSIX threads
Distinguish between processes and threads in terms of resource sharing

lecture_notes.introduction: A process is a running program. Threads allow a single process to do multiple things concurrently, sharing memory. Understanding processes and threads is essential for writing concurrent software that exploits modern multi-core CPUs.
key_concepts: Process model: address space, PCB, process states (new, ready, running, waiting, terminated)
Process creation: fork and exec on Unix
Context switching: saving and restoring register state
Threads: shared address space, independent stacks and registers
POSIX threads (pthreads): pthread_create, pthread_join, pthread_exit
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a C program that creates multiple threads to compute partial sums of a large array in parallel, then joins them to combine results

review.value: Processes have separate address spaces and are isolated; threads share the same address space and cooperate. Thread creation is cheaper because no memory space duplication is needed
Switching between processes requires saving all registers, switching page tables, and flushing TLB entries; thread switches within the same process skip the page table and TLB steps

learning_objectives: Identify race conditions and critical sections in concurrent code
Implement mutual exclusion using mutexes and semaphores
Solve classic synchronization problems: producer-consumer, readers-writers

lecture_notes.introduction: Concurrent programming is treacherous. Shared mutable state plus interleaved execution creates race conditions that appear randomly and are nearly impossible to reproduce. Synchronization primitives are the tools that make concurrent code correct.
key_concepts: Race condition: outcome depends on thread scheduling order
Critical section: code that accesses shared data
Mutex: mutual exclusion lock, pthread_mutex_lock and unlock
Semaphore: generalized lock with a counter, sem_wait and sem_post
Condition variables: pthread_cond_wait and signal for event notification
Deadlock conditions: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
lecture_notes.demonstration: Start with a buggy bank transfer program that has a race condition, demonstrate the bug with repeated runs, then fix it with mutexes, and finally show a deadlock scenario
setup_requirements: Linux environment with GCC and pthreads
Thread sanitizer enabled with fsanitize flag

review.value: Breaking any one of the four Coffman conditions prevents deadlock; the most practical approach is imposing a global lock ordering to prevent circular wait
Always check the condition in a while loop not an if, because spurious wakeups can occur and another thread may have already consumed the resource between signal and wakeup

learning_objectives: Compare CPU scheduling algorithms: FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, MLFQ
Calculate turnaround time, waiting time, and response time
Understand the design trade-offs between throughput and responsiveness

lecture_notes.introduction: The CPU scheduler decides which process runs next. This decision profoundly affects user experience: a bad scheduler makes your laptop feel sluggish even with plenty of CPU available. Understanding scheduling explains why some systems feel fast and others feel slow.
key_concepts: FCFS: simple but causes convoy effect
SJF: optimal average waiting time but requires prediction
Round Robin: time quantum, good response time, higher turnaround
MLFQ: adapts to process behavior dynamically
Real-time scheduling: rate-monotonic and earliest deadline first
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulate four scheduling algorithms on the same set of 6 processes, calculate all metrics, and compare using a Gantt chart visualization
setup_requirements: Process arrival and burst time data provided

review.value: In FCFS, a single long-running CPU-bound process can block many short IO-bound processes, severely degrading average waiting time; this motivates preemptive scheduling
Smaller time quantum gives better response time but increases context switch overhead; larger quantum approaches FCFS behavior. Typical values are 10 to 100 milliseconds

learning_objectives: Explain how virtual memory provides process isolation and the illusion of large memory
Trace address translation through page tables and TLB
Compare page replacement algorithms: FIFO, LRU, Clock
readings: OSTEP: Address Spaces, Paging, Page Replacement

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual memory is the OS mechanism that gives each process the illusion of having its own large, contiguous address space. It enables process isolation, shared libraries, and running programs larger than physical memory.
key_concepts: Virtual vs physical addresses: every process has its own address space
Page tables: mapping virtual pages to physical frames
TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer): caching page table entries
Page fault handling: loading pages from disk on demand
Page replacement: FIFO, LRU, Clock (second chance) algorithms
Thrashing: when the working set exceeds physical memory
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace a sequence of virtual addresses through a page table, calculating TLB hit and miss and page fault rates, then compare FIFO and LRU replacement on the same reference string

review.value: Pages are loaded into physical memory only when accessed on page fault, not when the process starts; this reduces startup time and memory usage but introduces page fault latency
With FIFO replacement, increasing the number of physical frames can sometimes increase page faults which is counterintuitive; LRU does not suffer from this anomaly

questions.question: A program creates 3 threads that each increment a shared counter 1,000,000 times. Without synchronization, what is the likely result and why?
questions.answer: The final counter value will be less than 3,000,000 and non-deterministic. Incrementing a counter is not atomic: it involves load, add, and store. When two threads load the same value before either stores, one increment is lost. The severity depends on thread scheduling interleaving.

rubric.key: States result will be less than 3,000,000

questions.question: Write pseudocode for a producer-consumer solution using a mutex and two semaphores (empty and full) for a bounded buffer of size N.
questions.answer:
Initialization: mutex = 1, empty = N, full = 0

Producer:
  while true:
    item = produce()
    sem_wait(empty)
    mutex_lock(mutex)
    buffer.add(item)
    mutex_unlock(mutex)
    sem_post(full)

Consumer:
  while true:
    sem_wait(full)
    mutex_lock(mutex)
    item = buffer.remove()
    mutex_unlock(mutex)
    sem_post(empty)
    consume(item)

rubric.key: Correct ordering: semaphore wait before mutex lock
Both producer and consumer correctly symmetric

questions.question: Given these processes with arrival time and burst time: P1(0,8), P2(1,4), P3(2,2), P4(3,1), calculate the average waiting time for Shortest Remaining Time First scheduling.
questions.answer:
Execution order with SRTF:
t=0: P1 starts (remaining=8)
t=1: P2 arrives (remaining=4 < P1 remaining 7), preempt, run P2
t=2: P3 arrives (remaining=2 < P2 remaining 3), preempt, run P3
t=3: P4 arrives (remaining=1 < P2 remaining 3), P3 done at t=4
t=4: P4 runs (remaining=1), finishes t=5
t=5: P2 resumes (remaining=3), finishes t=8
t=8: P1 resumes (remaining=7), finishes t=15

Waiting times: P1=15-0-8=7, P2=8-1-4=3, P3=4-2-2=0, P4=5-3-1=1
Average = (7+3+0+1)/4 = 2.75

rubric.key: Correct preemption decisions at each arrival
Correct completion times for all processes

questions.question: Given the reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 and 3 physical frames, calculate the number of page faults using LRU replacement.
questions.answer:
LRU trace with 3 frames:
1: [1] miss (1)
2: [1,2] miss (2)
3: [1,2,3] miss (3)
4: [2,3,4] miss, evict 1 LRU (4)
1: [3,4,1] miss, evict 2 LRU (5)
2: [4,1,2] miss, evict 3 LRU (6)
5: [1,2,5] miss, evict 4 LRU (7)
1: [2,5,1] hit
2: [5,1,2] hit
3: [1,2,3] miss, evict 5 LRU (8)
4: [2,3,4] miss, evict 1 LRU (9)
5: [3,4,5] miss, evict 2 LRU (10)

Total page faults: 10

major_assignments.title: Multi-threaded Web Server
major_assignments.description: Build a multi-threaded HTTP server in C that handles concurrent client connections using a thread pool. Implement proper synchronization for shared data structures, handle signals gracefully, and include performance benchmarking.
objectives: Apply process and thread concepts to build a concurrent network server
Implement a thread pool with work queue using mutexes and condition variables
Handle edge cases: connection timeouts, malformed requests, graceful shutdown
Benchmark throughput under varying concurrency levels
deliverables: C source code for the multi-threaded web server
Makefile with build, test, and clean targets
Performance benchmark results at 1, 10, 50, and 100 concurrent connections
Design document (2-3 pages) explaining thread pool architecture and synchronization

criteria: Server correctly serves static files over HTTP 1.1
Multiple clients can connect and be served concurrently
Thread pool distributes work across worker threads
No race conditions or memory leaks verified with sanitizer and valgrind
Work queue uses mutex and condition variable correctly
No deadlocks under any tested workload
Graceful shutdown: signal handler sets flag, workers drain queue and exit
Shared statistics such as request count and bytes served are synchronized
Malformed HTTP requests return appropriate error codes
Connection timeouts are handled without blocking the thread pool
Server recovers from individual connection failures
Resource limits for max connections and max file size are enforced
Design document explains architecture and synchronization strategy
Benchmark methodology is described and results are analyzed
Code is well-commented with clear function documentation
Makefile is complete and functional
Correct multi-threaded HTTP server with thread pool
Race-condition-free synchronization verified by sanitizer
Robust error handling for network and protocol edge cases
Clear documentation with performance benchmark analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of OS concepts and calculations

lab_assignments.description: Systems programming labs in C with pthreads and system calls

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering processes, threads, and synchronization

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all OS topics including memory and file systems

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Systems programming bugs discovered after submission may be addressed in office hours.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of OS concepts and debugging approaches is encouraged. All code must be written individually. Sharing code constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Using code from the internet, textbooks, or other students without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a Linux environment (VM or WSL2), GCC, make, valgrind, and strace. Lab computers with Linux are available.
